entries.position_summary: Supports robust export controls as essential to manage proliferation risk and enable regulated nuclear trade; absent such controls, governments would likely impose blanket bans on many exports.
entries.quote: Without export controls in place, governments of these exporting states would determine that the risk of nuclear supply was too high and would ban, rather than control, many exports.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that robust export controls, alongside UNSCR 1540 measures, are essential to enable responsible international nuclear cooperation for peaceful purposes. It rejects claims that export controls hinder peaceful uses, asserting they prevent diversion, ensure peaceful end-use, and support economic development.
entries.quote: We also affirm that export controls, in addition to the tools advanced through UNSCR 1540, are necessary enablers for the responsible international exchange of nuclear technology for peaceful uses. We therefore reject efforts by some Parties to incorrectly imply that they are not. Export controls prevent diversion of sensitive technologies, ensure technology exported for peaceful uses remains in peaceful uses, and actually contribute to economic development.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that strategic trade controls and multilateral export control regimes are consistent with, and actually bolster confidence in, the NPT right to peaceful nuclear uses. It contends these controls do not impede international technology exchange or economic development.
entries.quote: consistent with, and in fact facilitate and enhance confidence in, the right of States Parties to develop and use nuclear energy and technology for peaceful purposes. Though there is ample rhetoric to the contrary, there is no evidence that strategic trade controls, including the multilateral export control regimes, hamper the international exchange of technology for peaceful uses or hinder any country’s economic development.

entries.position_summary: The United States commits significant voluntary funding and technical assistance to enable safe and secure access to peaceful nuclear energy, including hundreds of programs on safeguards, nuclear security, and export controls. It reports providing over a quarter-billion dollars in extra-budgetary support to IAEA safeguards since 2015 and expresses a strong commitment to expanding peaceful uses.
entries.quote: In each of these areas, the United States devotes significant financial resources, technology, training and policy assistance on a voluntary basis to assure the fullest, secure and safe access to peaceful nuclear energy and technology. This includes hundreds of support programs conducted worldwide relating to nuclear safeguards, nuclear security, and export controls, as well as more than a quarter of a billion dollars of extrabudgetary IAEA safeguards support since the 2015 Review Conference. The United States is deeply committed to improving access to peaceful uses, and we look forward to discussing this further in our Cluster III statement.

entries.position_summary: The country urges full compliance with NPT safeguards and advocates making the IAEA Additional Protocol the global standard. It links responsible nuclear supply to adherence to these enhanced safeguards and calls for condemning violations.
entries.quote: Strengthening the NPT also requires that we address nonproliferation concerns openly, and thoroughly. Looking forward, our priority must be to condemn violations where they occur, insist on full compliance with NPT nonproliferation safeguards, and lift standards wherever possible, such as by making the Additional Protocol the standard for IAEA safeguards and for responsible nuclear supply.

entries.position_summary: The United States argues that robust nuclear export controls, consistent with Article III, are essential to nonproliferation and actually facilitate legitimate nuclear trade in line with NPT objectives. It highlights ongoing U.S. cooperation to update NSG Guidelines and Control Lists to address evolving technologies and proliferation risks.
entries.quote: Nuclear export controls, including those required under Article III, are another essential nonproliferation measure, necessary to ensure that nuclear-related transfers do not inadvertently contribute to the development of nuclear weapons. I wish to emphasize that nuclear export controls do not impede legitimate nuclear trade; in fact, they enable it and ensure consistency with the NPT’s purposes. The United States has long been a leader in this area, and continues to work with partners to update NSG Guidelines and Control Lists so that they remain effective in the face of evolving trade and technological developments and proliferation concerns.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the IAEA Additional Protocol as essential to achieving the Treaty’s safeguards objectives and advocates its universal adoption. It further argues the AP should be the standard/condition for nuclear supply by exporters.
entries.quote: safeguards objectives of the Treaty. The AP should also be a standard for nuclear supply.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates robust, responsible nuclear export controls as essential to nonproliferation and to enabling the fullest possible exchange for peaceful nuclear uses. It underscores the role of UNSCR 1540 in requiring domestic legal and regulatory measures to prevent nuclear terrorism, supporting strong national export control frameworks.
entries.quote: Chair, Responsible nuclear export controls are essential to effective nonproliferation and to facilitating the “fullest possible exchange” of nuclear material, equipment, and technology for peaceful purposes. Through Resolution 1540, export controls also help prevent nuclear terrorism by obligating States Parties to institute domestic legal- regulatory measures and controls. Robust export controls are thoroughly

entries.position_summary: The United States commits to the complete denuclearization of the DPRK in close coordination with allies, particularly the ROK and Japan. It supports full implementation of UN Security Council resolutions and enhanced monitoring to counter sanctions evasion that enables North Korea’s nuclear and missile programs.
entries.quote: The United States will pursue the complete denuclearization of North Korea, consulting closely with the Republic of Korea, Japan, and other allies and partners to achieve this goal and deter DPRK aggression. We will continue to cooperate with our allies and partners to fully implement UN Security Council resolutions and monitor the sanctions evasion which enables the DPRK’s unlawful nuclear and ballistic missile programs and destabilizing behavior.

entries.position_summary: The United States affirms the inalienable right to peaceful nuclear energy consistent with NPT Articles I and II, emphasizing nuclear power’s role in meeting global energy needs and positioning itself as a leading supplier. It stresses that NPT-facilitated cooperation should be contingent on adherence to the highest nonproliferation standards to ensure safety and security, implying robust safeguards and supply conditions.
entries.quote: The United States acknowledges the inalienable right to research, production and use of nuclear energy for peaceful purposes in conformity with NPT obligations under Articles I and II and we continue to emphasize that nuclear energy will significantly contribute to the world’s energy needs, with the United States ready to be the preeminent supplier. The NPT facilitates peaceful nuclear cooperation, and conforming with the highest nonproliferation standards will ensure this cooperation is safe and secure.

entries.position_summary: The United States denies any nuclear cooperation with Israel, stating that U.S. law prohibits such cooperation. This underscores adherence to U.S. export control and legal restrictions regarding nuclear engagements with Israel.
entries.quote: Finally, despite the claims of some in the hall, I wish to note the United States has no nuclear cooperation with Israel, nor could we under U.S. law.
